info.title: Code review fixes: MCP client leaks and argument marshaling

parts.text:
Verify each finding against the current code and only fix it if needed.

Inline comments:
In `@cmd/run.go`:
- Around line 298-316: The MCP client is left open when Register succeeds but
returns zero routed tools (filtered is empty); after calling
mcpRouter.Register(client, mcpTools, builtinNames) and before appending filtered
to req.Tools, detect if len(filtered) == 0 and close the client (call
client.Close()) to avoid the leak—do this in the same block where you currently
handle verbose logging and tool registration (referencing mcpRouter.Register,
filtered, client, builtinNames, req.Tools) so clients that register no tools are
explicitly closed.
- Around line 314-315: The loop that sets builtinNames[t.Name] = true is
incorrectly promoting MCP tool names to builtinNames; change the logic in the
loop that iterates over filtered so it skips tools that are MCP (e.g., check a
boolean like t.IsMCP or a kind/type field such as t.Kind == "mcp") and only add
true for genuine built-ins, so that internal/mcpclient.Router.Register can
detect and raise MCP/MCP collisions instead of them being treated as built-ins.
- Around line 210-211: The current code creates a single run-level timeout using
ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(),
time.Duration(timeout)*time.Second) which bypasses Cobra's cmd.Context() and
makes --timeout apply to the whole run; change to derive per-request deadlines
from cmd.Context() instead: remove the long-lived context.WithTimeout call based
on context.Background() and, at each network/request call site, wrap
cmd.Context() with context.WithTimeout(cmd.Context(),
time.Duration(timeout)*time.Second) and defer the returned cancel immediately
after the request scope so each round-trip gets its own deadline while
preserving Cobra cancellation propagation (update any references to ctx/cancel
accordingly).

In `@internal/envinterp/envinterp.go`:
- Around line 18-21: Wrap the error returned by expandValue with the header key
so callers (like Connect) get actionable feedback; inside the headers loop where
you call expandValue(value) change the return of err to a wrapped error that
includes the header name (e.g., using fmt.Errorf or errors.Wrapf) so the error
message identifies which header key caused the expansion failure.

In `@internal/mcpclient/mcpclient.go`:
- Around line 85-90: The request spotlights that CallTool currently forwards all
arguments as strings (producing e.g. "3" instead of 3) because schemas parsed in
ListTools aren't cached; fix by caching tool schemas on the Client (add a field
like Client.toolSchemas map[string]Schema populated in ListTools when calling
convertTool) and perform schema-aware coercion in Client.CallTool before
invoking session.CallTool: look up the stored schema for call.Name, coerce each
entry in the args map to the declared type (integer/number/boolean/string) using
the same conversion logic as convertTool, and then pass the coerced args into
mcp.CallToolParams; alternatively, if you prefer deferring coercion, ensure the
code path that has schema access (e.g., wherever convertTool is called) performs
the coercion and hands CallTool a typed args map rather than raw strings.

In `@internal/mcpclient/router.go`:
- Around line 11-17: Router.Close only iterates r.tools which omits clients that
registered zero routed tools, leaking MCP sessions; modify registration logic so
every Client is recorded for shutdown regardless of tool filtering (e.g., add
the Client to r.tools or a new r.clients map at the start of Register or
maintain a separate allClients set keyed by client ID) and ensure
Deregister/Close iterate that complete collection to call Client.Close/cleanup;
update Register, Close (and any Deregister/remove paths) to use the chosen
collection so clients that reported no routed tools are still closed.

---

Nitpick comments:
In `@cmd/run_mcp_integration_test.go`:
- Around line 21-33: The test MCP tool currently returns a fixed response
("hello axe") without inspecting the incoming request, which hides regressions
in argument marshaling; update the s.AddTool handler (the func registered for
mcp.Tool in cmd/run_mcp_integration_test.go that takes context.Context and
*mcp.CallToolRequest and returns *mcp.CallToolResult, error) to parse the
incoming *mcp.CallToolRequest (inspect/marshal/unmarshal the request.Arguments
or payload to extract the "name" field), validate or assert the value, and then
return a response that echoes that extracted name in the mcp.CallToolResult
Content instead of always returning "hello axe" so the test verifies end-to-end
argument forwarding.

In `@internal/envinterp/envinterp_test.go`:
- Around line 8-163: Combine the many TestExpandHeaders_* functions into a
single table-driven test TestExpandHeaders that iterates a slice of cases (each
with fields: name, env map[string]string, input map[string]string or nil, want
map[string]string or nil, wantErr bool, wantErrMsg string) and uses
t.Run(case.name, func(t *testing.T){...}) to set env vars via t.Setenv, call
ExpandHeaders, assert error vs expected and compare results (including
preserving the original input for the "InputNotModified" case and handling nil
vs empty maps for "NilMap" and "EmptyMap"), and include specific cases from the
originals (StaticValues, SingleVar, MultipleVarsInOneValue, MultipleHeaders,
MissingVar, EmptyVar, NilMap, EmptyMap, NoPattern, DollarWithoutBrace,
InputNotModified) so behavior and assertions remain identical but condensed into
subtests.

In `@internal/mcpclient/mcpclient_test.go`:
- Around line 16-249: Convert the many similar tests in
internal/mcpclient/mcpclient_test.go (Connect, ListTools, CallTool, Header
injection and env-var failure cases) into table-driven tests: create tables of
test cases with fields like name, serverSetup (use newMCPStreamableServer /
newMCPComplexSchemaServer / inline mcp.NewServer setups), cfg
(agent.MCPServerConfig), prepare (e.g., t.Setenv for TOKEN),
expectErr/expectResult, and expected assertions; iterate with for _, tc := range
tests { t.Run(tc.name, func(t *testing.T) { setup server via tc.serverSetup,
defer ts.Close(), client := Connect/mustConnectTestClient using tc.cfg, defer
client.Close(); perform ListTools or CallTool and assert tc.expectErr or compare
results }) } and keep unique checks such as Authorization header inspection and
complex-schema assertions in their case entries; preserve cleanup and error
messages and use subtests to make each case independent.
I'll start by reading all the files mentioned in the findings to verify each one against the current code.

417: 			assistantMsg := provider.Message{
418: 				Role:      "assistant",
419: 				Content:   resp.Content,
420: 				ToolCalls: resp.ToolCalls,
421: 			}
422: 			req.Messages = append(req.Messages, assistantMsg)
423: 
424: 			// Execute tool calls
425: 			results := executeToolCalls(ctx, resp.ToolCalls, cfg, globalCfg, registry, mcpRouter, depth, effectiveMaxDepth, parallel, verbose, cmd.ErrOrStderr(), workdir)
426: 			totalToolCalls += len(resp.ToolCalls)
427: 
428: 			if jsonOutput {
429: 				for i, tc := range resp.ToolCalls {
430: 					input := tc.Arguments
431: 					if input == nil {
432: 						input = map[string]string{}
433: 					}
434: 
435: 					allToolCallDetails = append(allToolCallDetails, toolCallDetail{
436: 						Name:    tc.Name,
437: 						Input:   input,
438: 						Output:  truncateOutput(results[i].Content),
439: 						IsError: results[i].IsError,
440: 					})
441: 				}
442: 			}
443: 
444: 			// Append tool result message
445: 			toolMsg := provider.Message{
446: 				Role:        "tool",
447: 				ToolResults: results,
448: 			}
449: 			req.Messages = append(req.Messages, toolMsg)
450: 		}
451: 
452: 		// Check if we exhausted turns
453: 		if resp != nil && len(resp.ToolCalls) > 0 {
454: 			return &ExitError{Code: 1, Err: fmt.Errorf("agent exceeded maximum conversation turns (%d)", maxConversationTurns)}
455: 		}
456: 
457: 		if verbose {
458: 			durationMs := time.Since(start).Milliseconds()
459: 			_, _ = fmt.Fprintf(cmd.ErrOrStderr(), "Duration: %dms\n", durationMs)
460: 			_, _ = fmt.Fprintf(cmd.ErrOrStderr(), "Tokens:   %d input, %d output (cumulative)\n", totalInputTokens, totalOutputTokens)
461: 			_, _ = fmt.Fprintf(cmd.ErrOrStderr(), "Stop:     %s\n", resp.StopReason)
462: 		}
463: 	}
464: 
465: 	durationMs := time.Since(start).Milliseconds()
466: 
467: 	// Step 19: JSON output
468: 	if jsonOutput {
469: 		if allToolCallDetails == nil {
470: 			allToolCallDetails = make([]toolCallDetail, 0)
471: 		}
472: 
473: 		envelope := map[string]interface{}{
474: 			"model":             resp.Model,
475: 			"content":           resp.Content,
476: 			"input_tokens":      totalInputTokens,
477: 			"output_tokens":     totalOutputTokens,
478: 			"stop_reason":       resp.StopReason,
479: 			"duration_ms":       durationMs,
480: 			"tool_calls":        totalToolCalls,
481: 			"tool_call_details": allToolCallDetails,
482: 			"refused":           refusal.Detect(resp.Content),
483: 		}
484: 		data, err := json.Marshal(envelope)
485: 		if err != nil {
486: 			return &ExitError{Code: 1, Err: fmt.Errorf("failed to marshal JSON output: %w", err)}
487: 		}
488: 		_, _ = fmt.Fprintln(cmd.OutOrStdout(), string(data))
489: 	} else {
490: 		// Step 20: Default output
491: 		_, _ = fmt.Fprint(cmd.OutOrStdout(), resp.Content)
492: 	}
493: 
494: 	// Step 21: Append memory entry after successful response
495: 	if cfg.Memory.Enabled {
496: 		appendPath, appendErr := memory.FilePath(agentName, cfg.Memory.Path)
497: 		if appendErr != nil {
498: 			_, _ = fmt.Fprintf(cmd.ErrOrStderr(), "Warning: failed to save memory for %q: %v\n", agentName, appendErr)
499: 		} else {
500: 			if appendErr = memory.AppendEntry(appendPath, userMessage, resp.Content); appendErr != nil {
501: 				_, _ = fmt.Fprintf(cmd.ErrOrStderr(), "Warning: failed to save memory for %q: %v\n", agentName, appendErr)
502: 			}
503: 		}
504: 	}
505: 
506: 	return nil
507: }

600: // executeToolCalls dispatches tool calls and returns results.
601: // When parallel is true and there are multiple calls, they run concurrently.
602: func executeToolCalls(ctx context.Context, toolCalls []provider.ToolCall, cfg *agent.AgentConfig, globalCfg *config.GlobalConfig, registry *tool.Registry, mcpRouter *mcpclient.Router, depth, maxDepth int, parallel, verbose bool, stderr io.Writer, workdir string) []provider.ToolResult {
603: 	results := make([]provider.ToolResult, len(toolCalls))
604: 
605: 	execOpts := tool.ExecuteOptions{
606: 		AllowedAgents: cfg.SubAgents,
607: 		ParentModel:   cfg.Model,
608: 		Depth:         depth,
609: 		MaxDepth:      maxDepth,
610: 		Timeout:       cfg.SubAgentsConf.Timeout,
611: 		GlobalConfig:  globalCfg,
612: 		MCPRouter:     mcpRouter,
613: 		Verbose:       verbose,
614: 		Stderr:        stderr,
615: 	}
616: 
617: 	if len(toolCalls) == 1 || !parallel {
618: 		// Sequential execution (also used for single call)
619: 		for i, tc := range toolCalls {
620: 			if mcpRouter != nil && mcpRouter.Has(tc.Name) {
621: 				results[i] = dispatchToolCall(ctx, tc, registry, mcpRouter, verbose, stderr, workdir)
622: 			} else if tc.Name == tool.CallAgentToolName {
623: 				results[i] = tool.ExecuteCallAgent(ctx, tc, execOpts)
624: 			} else {
625: 				results[i] = dispatchToolCall(ctx, tc, registry, mcpRouter, verbose, stderr, workdir)
626: 			}
627: 		}
628: 	} else {
629: 		// Parallel execution
630: 		type indexedResult struct {
631: 			index  int
632: 			result provider.ToolResult
633: 		}
634: 		ch := make(chan indexedResult, len(toolCalls))
635: 		for i, tc := range toolCalls {
636: 			go func(idx int, call provider.ToolCall) {
637: 				var res provider.ToolResult
638: 				if mcpRouter != nil && mcpRouter.Has(call.Name) {
639: 					res = dispatchToolCall(ctx, call, registry, mcpRouter, verbose, stderr, workdir)
640: 				} else if call.Name == tool.CallAgentToolName {
641: 					res = tool.ExecuteCallAgent(ctx, call, execOpts)
642: 				} else {
643: 					res = dispatchToolCall(ctx, call, registry, mcpRouter, verbose, stderr, workdir)
644: 				}
645: 				ch <- indexedResult{index: idx, result: res}
646: 			}(i, tc)
647: 		}
648: 		for range toolCalls {
649: 			ir := <-ch
650: 			results[ir.index] = ir.result
651: 		}
652: 	}
653: 
654: 	return results
655: }
656: 
657: func dispatchToolCall(ctx context.Context, tc provider.ToolCall, registry *tool.Registry, mcpRouter *mcpclient.Router, verbose bool, stderr io.Writer, workdir string) provider.ToolResult {
658: 	if mcpRouter != nil && mcpRouter.Has(tc.Name) {
659: 		if verbose && stderr != nil {
660: 			if serverName, ok := mcpRouter.ServerName(tc.Name); ok {
661: 				_, _ = fmt.Fprintf(stderr, "[mcp] Routing tool %q to server %q\n", tc.Name, serverName)
662: 			}
663: 		}
664: 		result, err := mcpRouter.Dispatch(ctx, tc)
665: 		if err != nil {
666: 			return provider.ToolResult{CallID: tc.ID, Content: err.Error(), IsError: true}
667: 		}
668: 		return result
669: 	}
670: 
671: 	result, dispatchErr := registry.Dispatch(ctx, tc, tool.ExecContext{Workdir: workdir, Stderr: stderr, Verbose: verbose})
672: 	if dispatchErr != nil {
673: 		return provider.ToolResult{CallID: tc.ID, Content: dispatchErr.Error(), IsError: true}
674: 	}
675: 	return result
676: }

678: // mapProviderError converts a provider error to an ExitError with the correct exit code.
679: func mapProviderError(err error) error {
680: 	var provErr *provider.ProviderError
681: 	if errors.As(err, &provErr) {
682: 		switch provErr.Category {
683: 		case provider.ErrCategoryAuth, provider.ErrCategoryRateLimit,
684: 			provider.ErrCategoryTimeout, provider.ErrCategoryOverloaded,
685: 			provider.ErrCategoryServer:
686: 			return &ExitError{Code: 3, Err: provErr}
687: 		case provider.ErrCategoryBadRequest:
688: 			return &ExitError{Code: 1, Err: provErr}
689: 		}
690: 	}
691: 	return &ExitError{Code: 1, Err: err}
692: }

20: 		if err != nil {
21: 			return nil, err
22: 		}
23: 		expanded[key] = replaced
24: 	}

29: func expandValue(value string) (string, error) {
30: 	matches := envPattern.FindAllStringSubmatch(value, -1)
31: 	if len(matches) == 0 {
32: 		return value, nil
33: 	}
34: 
35: 	var expandErr error
36: 	replaced := envPattern.ReplaceAllStringFunc(value, func(token string) string {
37: 		if expandErr != nil {
38: 			return token
39: 		}
40: 		parts := envPattern.FindStringSubmatch(token)
41: 		if len(parts) != 2 {
42: 			return token
43: 		}
44: 		name := parts[1]
45: 		v := os.Getenv(name)
46: 		if v == "" {
47: 			expandErr = fmt.Errorf("environment variable %q is not set or empty", name)
48: 			return token

50: 		return v

37: 	default:
38: 		return nil, fmt.Errorf("unsupported MCP transport %q", cfg.Transport)
39: 	}
40: 
41: 	client := mcp.NewClient(&mcp.Implementation{Name: "axe", Version: "1.0.0"}, nil)
42: 	session, err := client.Connect(ctx, transport, nil)
43: 	if err != nil {
44: 		return nil, fmt.Errorf("connect MCP server %q: %w", cfg.Name, err)

111: func convertTool(tool *mcp.Tool) provider.Tool {

113: 		Name:        tool.Name,
114: 		Description: tool.Description,
115: 		Parameters:  map[string]provider.ToolParameter{},
116: 	}
117: 
118: 	schema, ok := tool.InputSchema.(map[string]any)
119: 	if !ok {
120: 		return out
121: 	}
122: 
123: 	required := map[string]bool{}
124: 	if reqList, ok := schema["required"].([]any); ok {
125: 		for _, item := range reqList {
126: 			if name, ok := item.(string); ok {
127: 				required[name] = true
128: 			}
129: 		}
130: 	} else if reqList, ok := schema["required"].([]string); ok {
131: 		for _, name := range reqList {
132: 			required[name] = true
133: 		}
134: 	}
135: 
136: 	properties, ok := schema["properties"].(map[string]any)
137: 	if !ok {
138: 		return out
139: 	}
140: 
141: 	for name, raw := range properties {
142: 		param := provider.ToolParameter{Type: "string", Required: required[name]}
143: 		property, ok := raw.(map[string]any)
144: 		if !ok {
145: 			param.Description = "Type unknown"
146: 			out.Parameters[name] = param
147: 			continue
148: 		}
149: 
150: 		desc, _ := property["description"].(string)
151: 		if typeName, ok := property["type"].(string); ok {
152: 			switch typeName {
153: 			case "string", "integer", "number", "boolean":
154: 				param.Type = typeName
155: 				param.Description = desc
156: 			default:
157: 				param.Description = prependTypeDescription(typeName, desc)
158: 			}
159: 		} else {
160: 			param.Description = prependTypeDescription("unknown", desc)
161: 		}
162: 
163: 		out.Parameters[name] = param
164: 	}
165: 
166: 	return out
167: }

169: func prependTypeDescription(typeName, desc string) string {
170: 	if desc == "" {
171: 		return fmt.Sprintf("Type %s", typeName)
172: 	}
173: 	return fmt.Sprintf("Type %s: %s", typeName, desc)

27: 
28: 		if existing, ok := r.tools[tool.Name]; ok && existing != client {
29: 			return nil, fmt.Errorf("MCP tool %q collision between servers %q and %q", tool.Name, existing.Name(), client.Name())
30: 		}
31: 
32: 		r.tools[tool.Name] = client
33: 		filtered = append(filtered, tool)
34: 	}
35: 
36: 	return filtered, nil
37: }
38: 
39: // Dispatch routes a tool call to the correct MCP client.
40: func (r *Router) Dispatch(ctx context.Context, call provider.ToolCall) (provider.ToolResult, error) {
41: 	client, ok := r.tools[call.Name]
42: 	if !ok {
43: 		return provider.ToolResult{}, fmt.Errorf("unknown MCP tool %q", call.Name)
44: 	}
45: 	return client.CallTool(ctx, call)
46: }
47: 
48: // Has reports whether the router has a route for the given tool name.
49: func (r *Router) Has(name string) bool {
50: 	_, ok := r.tools[name]
51: 	return ok
52: }
53: 
54: // ServerName returns the MCP server name that owns a tool.
55: func (r *Router) ServerName(name string) (string, bool) {
56: 	client, ok := r.tools[name]
57: 	if !ok {
58: 		return "", false
59: 	}
60: 	return client.Name(), true
61: }
62: 
63: // Close closes each unique client once.
64: func (r *Router) Close() error {
65: 	closed := map[*Client]struct{}{}
66: 	var firstErr error
67: 
68: 	for _, client := range r.tools {
69: 		if _, seen := closed[client]; seen {
70: 			continue
71: 		}
72: 		closed[client] = struct{}{}
73: 		if err := client.Close(); err != nil && firstErr == nil {
74: 			firstErr = err
75: 		}
76: 	}
77: 
78: 	return firstErr
79: }

All three structs are defined in:

**`/Users/jaronswab/go/src/github.com/jrswab/axe/internal/provider/provider.go`**

---

## Struct Definitions

### `provider.Tool` (lines 34-38)

```go

	Name        string
	Description string
	Parameters  map[string]ToolParameter
}
```

### `provider.ToolCall` (lines 41-45)

### `provider.ToolResult` (lines 48-52)

```go
}
```

### Supporting type -- `provider.ToolParameter` (lines 27-31)

```go
// ToolParameter describes a single parameter of a tool definition.
type ToolParameter struct {
	Type        string
	Description string
	Required    bool
}
```

---

## MCP vs Built-in Tool Distinction

**The `provider.Tool` struct has NO field** like `IsMCP`, `Kind`, `Source`, or any other marker to distinguish MCP tools from built-in tools. The struct is intentionally flat and uniform.

Instead, the distinction between MCP and built-in tools is handled **at the routing/dispatch layer**, not on the struct itself. The mechanism works as follows:

1. **`mcpclient.Router`** (`/Users/jaronswab/go/src/github.com/jrswab/axe/internal/mcpclient/router.go`) maintains a `map[string]*Client` that maps tool names to their owning MCP client. It provides a `Has(name string) bool` method to check if a tool name belongs to an MCP server.

2. **`dispatchToolCall`** in `/Users/jaronswab/go/src/github.com/jrswab/axe/cmd/run.go` (line 657) uses the router to decide dispatch:
   - If `mcpRouter != nil && mcpRouter.Has(tc.Name)` is true, the call is routed to the MCP server via `mcpRouter.Dispatch(ctx, tc)`.
   - Otherwise, the call is dispatched to the built-in tool registry via `registry.Dispatch(ctx, tc, ...)`.

3. **Name-collision prevention**: During MCP tool registration (`Router.Register` at `router.go:21`), any MCP tool whose name collides with a built-in name (checked via `builtinNames map[string]bool`) is silently skipped, ensuring built-in tools always take precedence.

In summary, the `provider.Tool` type is a single, undifferentiated struct. The MCP-vs-builtin distinction lives entirely in which routing table (`mcpclient.Router` vs `tool.Registry`) knows about the tool name at dispatch time.

parts.text:
## Verification of All Findings

### Finding 1: MCP client leak when Register returns zero filtered tools

**Code** (`cmd/run.go:298-316`):
```go
filtered, registerErr := mcpRouter.Register(client, mcpTools, builtinNames)
if registerErr != nil {
    _ = client.Close()
    return &ExitError{Code: 2, ...}
}
// ... verbose logging ...
req.Tools = append(req.Tools, filtered...)
for _, t := range filtered {
    builtinNames[t.Name] = true
}
```

**Verdict: VALID but partially mitigated.** If `Register` succeeds but returns zero filtered tools, the client is NOT closed immediately. However, `mcpRouter.Close()` is deferred at line 271. The question is whether `Router.Close()` would close this client — and that depends on Finding 6. Since `Router.Close()` only iterates `r.tools` (which maps tool names to clients), a client with zero registered tools would indeed NOT appear in `r.tools` and would never be closed by `Router.Close()`. So **this is a real leak**, but it's intertwined with Finding 6.

**Fix needed: Yes.** Close the client when `len(filtered) == 0`.

---

### Finding 2: builtinNames incorrectly includes MCP tool names

**Code** (`cmd/run.go:314-315`):
```go
for _, t := range filtered {
    builtinNames[t.Name] = true
}
```

**Verdict: This is NOT a bug — it's actually correct behavior.** The `builtinNames` map is used to prevent collisions. After the first MCP server registers tools, those names are added to `builtinNames` so that if a second MCP server tries to register the same tool name, it gets skipped (treated as already taken). The actual MCP/MCP collision detection happens inside `Router.Register()` at `router.go:28-29` — it checks `r.tools[tool.Name]` for same-name tools from different clients and returns an error.

The name `builtinNames` is misleading (it should be something like `reservedNames` or `claimedNames`), but the logic is correct: a second MCP server with the same tool name will be silently skipped rather than erroring. The `Router.Register` collision check only fires when a tool passes the `builtinNames` filter. So the current behavior is: first-MCP-server wins silently, just like built-ins win silently.

If you want MCP/MCP collisions to error (as the finding suggests), you'd need to NOT add MCP tool names to `builtinNames` and let `Router.Register`'s collision check handle it. But this changes semantics: currently duplicates are silently skipped; the finding wants them to error. **This is a design decision, not a bug.**

**Fix needed: Debatable — design choice, not a bug.**

---

### Finding 3: context.WithTimeout bypasses cmd.Context()

**Code** (`cmd/run.go:210-211`):
```go
ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), time.Duration(timeout)*time.Second)
defer cancel()
```

**Verdict: The observation is correct** — using `context.Background()` means signals propagated through Cobra's `cmd.Context()` (e.g., SIGINT handling) are ignored. However, the proposed fix (per-request deadlines at each call site) is a significant architectural change.

Looking at the code, `ctx` is used for: MCP Connect (line 283), MCP ListTools (line 292), and `prov.Send` (lines 365, 395), plus `executeToolCalls` (line 425). The current single timeout covers the entire run, which seems intentional for a CLI that's meant to be an executor (per AGENTS.md: "Executor, not scheduler").

The proposed per-request approach would mean each individual Send/Connect gets its own full timeout, so a run with 10 tool-call rounds could take up to 10x the timeout. That seems worse for a CLI tool.

The real fix is simpler: derive from `cmd.Context()` instead of `context.Background()` to preserve signal propagation while keeping the single run-level timeout:
```go
ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(cmd.Context(), time.Duration(timeout)*time.Second)
```

**Fix needed: Yes, but simpler than proposed.** Just change `context.Background()` to `cmd.Context()`.

---

### Finding 4: ExpandHeaders error missing header key context

**Code** (`envinterp.go:18-21`):
```go
for key, value := range headers {
    replaced, err := expandValue(value)
    if err != nil {
        return nil, err
    }
```

**Verdict: VALID.** The error from `expandValue` says `environment variable "TOKEN" is not set or empty` but doesn't say which header key triggered it. When you have multiple headers with env vars, this is unhelpful.

**Fix needed: Yes.** Wrap with the header key name.

---

### Finding 5: CallTool forwards all args as strings

**Code** (`mcpclient.go:84-88`):
```go
func (c *Client) CallTool(ctx context.Context, call provider.ToolCall) (provider.ToolResult, error) {
    args := make(map[string]any, len(call.Arguments))
    for key, value := range call.Arguments {
        args[key] = value
    }
```

**Verdict: VALID.** `call.Arguments` is `map[string]string` (confirmed from `provider.ToolCall`). The loop copies each string value into `map[string]any`, but the values remain strings. An MCP tool expecting `{"count": 3}` will receive `{"count": "3"}`. The schema info from `convertTool` is discarded after `ListTools` returns.

**Fix needed: Yes.** Cache tool schemas and coerce arguments in `CallTool`.

---

### Finding 6: Router.Close misses clients with zero routed tools

**Code** (`router.go:64-78`):
```go
func (r *Router) Close() error {
    closed := map[*Client]struct{}{}
    for _, client := range r.tools {
```

**Verdict: VALID.** `r.tools` only contains entries for tools that passed the filter. If an MCP server registered but all its tools were filtered out (all collided with built-ins), that client won't be in `r.tools` and won't be closed.

Combined with Finding 1 (no close on `len(filtered)==0` in run.go), this is a confirmed session leak.

**Fix needed: Yes.** Add a separate `r.clients` slice/set to track all registered clients.

---

### Nitpick 1: MCP test handler ignores request arguments

**Code** (`run_mcp_integration_test.go:31-32`):
```go
}, func(_ context.Context, _ *mcp.CallToolRequest) (*mcp.CallToolResult, error) {
    return &mcp.CallToolResult{Content: []mcp.Content{&mcp.TextContent{Text: "hello axe"}}}, nil
```

**Verdict: VALID.** The handler ignores the request and always returns "hello axe". With Finding 5 (string coercion issue), this test can't detect argument marshaling bugs.

**Fix needed: Yes, worth doing**, especially since we'll be fixing argument coercion in Finding 5.

---

### Nitpick 2 & 3: Table-driven test refactoring

**Verdict: VALID but low priority.** Both test files use separate test functions instead of table-driven tests. This is a style preference.

**Fix needed: Optional.** These are pure style improvements.

---

## Summary & Plan

Here's my assessment of all 9 findings with a recommended fix plan:

### Confirmed bugs (fix):

| # | Finding | File | Severity | Fix |
|---|---------|------|----------|-----|
| 1 | MCP client leak when `len(filtered)==0` | `cmd/run.go:298-316` | High | Close client when `len(filtered)==0` after `Register` |
| 3 | `context.Background()` bypasses Cobra signals | `cmd/run.go:210` | High | Change to `cmd.Context()` — one-line fix, NOT the per-request approach the finding suggests |
| 4 | Error missing header key context | `envinterp.go:19-21` | Medium | Wrap error with `fmt.Errorf("header %q: %w", key, err)` |
| 5 | Args forwarded as strings, no coercion | `mcpclient.go:84-90` | High | Cache tool schemas in `Client`, coerce args in `CallTool` |
| 6 | `Router.Close` misses zero-tool clients | `router.go:11-78` | High | Add `r.clients` set, register all clients, iterate in `Close` |

### Confirmed nitpick (fix):

| # | Finding | File | Fix |
|---|---------|------|-----|
| N1 | Test handler ignores request arguments | `run_mcp_integration_test.go:31-32` | Echo the `name` field from request args |

### Not a bug / skip:

| # | Finding | Reason |
|---|---------|--------|
| 2 | `builtinNames` includes MCP names | **Not a bug.** The map prevents duplicate registration across MCP servers. The behavior (first-wins, silent skip) is consistent with how built-in collisions work. Renaming the variable would be clearer, but the logic is correct. |
| N2 | envinterp_test.go not table-driven | Style preference, not a defect. Tests are clear as-is. |
| N3 | mcpclient_test.go not table-driven | Style preference, not a defect. Tests are clear as-is. |

### Key disagreement with the review:

- **Finding 2**: The review claims `builtinNames` is "incorrectly promoting MCP tool names." It's actually correct — it prevents the second MCP server from silently overwriting the first server's tool route. Without this, two MCP servers with the same tool name would silently give the tool to the *first* server (via `Router.Register`'s collision check) while the second server's tool *also* gets appended to `req.Tools`, creating a phantom tool that can never be dispatched. The map prevents that.

- **Finding 3**: The review proposes per-request deadlines at each call site. That's overly complex and changes semantics (total run time becomes unbounded). The real issue is `context.Background()` vs `cmd.Context()` — a one-line fix.

### Execution order:

1. **Finding 6** (Router tracks all clients) — prerequisite for Finding 1
2. **Finding 1** (Close client on zero filtered tools) — with Finding 6 done, this can alternatively just rely on Router.Close, but an explicit close is still good defensive coding
3. **Finding 5** (Schema-aware coercion) + **Nitpick 1** (test echoes args) — do together
4. **Finding 3** (context fix) — one-line change
5. **Finding 4** (error wrapping) — one-line change

parts.text:
Good point. Let me think about this more carefully.

The current code uses `builtinNames` for two distinct purposes:
1. **Prevent built-in/MCP collisions** — built-in tools always win (silent skip)
2. **Prevent MCP/MCP collisions** — first MCP server wins (silent skip)

But `Router.Register` at `router.go:28-29` already has explicit MCP/MCP collision detection:
```go
if existing, ok := r.tools[tool.Name]; ok && existing != client {
    return nil, fmt.Errorf("MCP tool %q collision between servers %q and %q", ...)
}
```

This collision check is currently **dead code for cross-server collisions** because `builtinNames` catches MCP tool names first and silently skips them before they ever reach the router's collision check. The router's error path only fires if the *same* server somehow registers the same tool twice (unlikely).

So the separation of concerns argument is:
- `builtinNames` should only contain actual built-in tool names — its job is "protect built-ins"
- `Router.Register` should handle MCP/MCP conflicts — its job is "manage MCP routing"
- Currently `builtinNames` is doing both jobs, which masks the router's collision detection

If we stop adding MCP names to `builtinNames`, then:
- Built-in/MCP conflicts: still silently skipped (correct, via `builtinNames`)
- MCP/MCP conflicts: Router.Register returns an error (clearer failure mode)

The risk: is it better to error on MCP/MCP duplicate tool names or silently give it to the first server? For a CLI that should "print nothing" and have "errors that help the user fix the problem," erroring with a clear collision message seems more aligned with the project philosophy.

One caveat: removing MCP names from `builtinNames` means the second server's duplicate tool would NOT be silently skipped — it would hit `Router.Register`, which would return an error and abort the run. Currently it silently skips. That's a behavior change, but arguably the correct one: if two MCP servers export the same tool name, the user should know about it rather than have one silently shadow the other.

- **Before**: If two MCP servers both export tool "search", the second server's tool is silently skipped (first-wins)
- **After**: Axe returns an error: `MCP tool "search" collision between servers "server-a" and "server-b"`

This is a user-facing behavior change. The error is more helpful (aligns with project philosophy), but it could break configs that currently happen to work because duplicates are silently ignored. Should I include this fix?

parts.text:
### Implementation Plan

**1. `internal/mcpclient/router.go` — Fix 6: Track all clients**
- Add `clients []*Client` field to `Router` struct
- In `Register`, append the client to `r.clients` unconditionally at the start
- In `Close`, iterate `r.clients` instead of `r.tools` to ensure all clients are closed
- Write test: register a client with zero routed tools, verify `Close` still closes it

**2. `cmd/run.go` — Fix 1: Close client on empty filtered**
- After `mcpRouter.Register(...)` succeeds, if `len(filtered) == 0`, the router now tracks it (from Fix 6), so the deferred `mcpRouter.Close()` handles it. No explicit close needed in run.go. However, for defense-in-depth and to free resources sooner, we could still log a verbose message. Actually, since Fix 6 handles it, this is already resolved. I'll add a verbose log noting zero tools registered.

**3. `cmd/run.go` — Fix 2: Remove MCP names from builtinNames**
- Delete the loop at lines 314-316 that adds `filtered` tool names to `builtinNames`
- MCP/MCP collisions will now be caught by `Router.Register`'s existing collision check

**4. `internal/mcpclient/mcpclient.go` — Fix 5: Schema-aware arg coercion**
- Add `toolSchemas map[string]map[string]string` field to `Client` (maps tool name -> param name -> type)
- Populate in `ListTools` from `convertTool` output
- In `CallTool`, look up schema, coerce each arg: `"integer"` → `strconv.Atoi`, `"number"` → `strconv.ParseFloat`, `"boolean"` → `strconv.ParseBool`, `"string"` → keep as-is
- Write test: MCP server with integer/boolean params, verify coerced values arrive correctly

**5. `cmd/run.go:210` — Fix 3: Use cmd.Context()**
- Change `context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), ...)` to `context.WithTimeout(cmd.Context(), ...)`

**6. `internal/envinterp/envinterp.go:19-21` — Fix 4: Wrap error**
- Change `return nil, err` to `return nil, fmt.Errorf("header %q: %w", key, err)`
- Update existing test assertion for `MissingVar` and `EmptyVar` to expect the wrapped message

**7. `cmd/run_mcp_integration_test.go` — Nitpick 1: Echo args**
- Update the handler to extract `request.Arguments["name"]` and return it in the response
- Update test assertion to verify the echoed name

**8. Run all tests** to verify nothing breaks.

parts.text:
All 7 fixes implemented and verified. Full test suite passes (14 packages, 0 failures).

## Summary of changes

### `internal/mcpclient/router.go` — Fix 6 (client leak on Close)
- Added `clients []*Client` field to `Router` struct
- `Register` now appends every client to `r.clients` unconditionally before filtering
- `Close` iterates `r.clients` instead of `r.tools`, ensuring zero-tool clients get closed

### `cmd/run.go` — Fixes 2 & 3
- **Fix 2** (line 314): Removed the loop that added MCP tool names to `builtinNames`. MCP/MCP collisions are now caught by `Router.Register`'s existing collision check with a clear error message, instead of being silently swallowed.
- **Fix 3** (line 210): Changed `context.Background()` to `cmd.Context()` so the run-level timeout respects Cobra's signal propagation (SIGINT, etc.)

### `internal/mcpclient/mcpclient.go` — Fix 5 (string argument coercion)
- Added `toolSchemas map[string]map[string]string` field to `Client`
- `ListTools` now caches parameter type info from each tool's schema
- `CallTool` uses `coerceArg()` to convert string arguments to their declared types (integer/number/boolean) before forwarding to the MCP server
- Added `coerceArg()` helper with graceful fallback to string on parse failure

### `internal/envinterp/envinterp.go` — Fix 4 (error context)
- `ExpandHeaders` now wraps expansion errors with the header key name: `header "Authorization": environment variable "X" is not set or empty`

### `cmd/run_mcp_integration_test.go` — Nitpick 1 (argument forwarding)
- MCP test handler now extracts and echoes the `"name"` argument from the request instead of returning a hardcoded response, validating end-to-end argument forwarding

### Test files updated
- `internal/mcpclient/router_test.go` — New `TestRouter_Close_ClosesZeroToolClients`; fixed 3 existing tests to populate `r.clients`
- `internal/mcpclient/mcpclient_test.go` — New `TestCoerceArg` (11 subtests), `TestCallTool_CoercesArgTypes`, `newMCPTypedArgsServer`
- `internal/envinterp/envinterp_test.go` — Updated 2 error assertions to match wrapped format
